verbatim_text: They're pre-training their models at about 20 tokens per parameter. They're pretty much using this recipe. And what this plot shows here is you have your model size on the x-axis and your loss on the y-axis, and then you have a bunch of these hyperbolic kind of curves. So each one of these hyperbolas is basically what you do if you have a fixed compute budget, and you're trying to decide how to allocate your data set size and your model size. And you can make different decisions. It's a trade-off. As you train on more data, you can't train as large of a model because you've spent more of your compute on data, and vice versa. If you train on much less data, you can afford to train a much larger model. And so there's an optimal trade-off somewhere in the middle, and that's called the compute optimum. And as you trace out how this compute optimum evolves across several different compute scales, you can estimate the forecast you saw a couple of thoughts ago. And then you can use that forecast to extrapolate to much more expensive, much higher compute scales and figure out what the rate we change that. Okay, so that's great, but this maybe misses some things in the more modern context. So now we've moved to coding models and math models, and everyone is now doing reasoning with their models. And so maybe we should revisit Chinchilla, because if we did this pre-training and it wasn't considering reasoning at the time, Chinchilla might actually be suboptimal.
